author: @GrayHand-jz6ie
text:
An idea to try. Making it rain anywhere with electrostatic water condensation. Kind of amazed this isn't done everywhere. I priced it out and stainless steel mesh is cheap enough you can make one of these cheaper than running water lines and pumps and it has no moving parts. Here's the thing, if there's any humidity the system will work and that includes using brine or ocean water to raise humidity. I planned to use black inflatable domes in the ocean. It would be an amazing system for Saudi Arabia. Unlimited water.

Okay, a chimpanzee can build one and if a chimp isn;t handy have a kid build one as a science fair project. Two stainless steel screens, say a 1/4" apart so use plastic or wood as a spacer, just needs to be non conductive. Believe it or not you are almost done. Hook up a neon transformer. I can;t remember but switch poles until moisture collects on the lower screen, pretty sure it's positive the water ions migrate to. You see water ions move between the poles and collect on the one wire and they will collect quite fast. Essentially depending on ambient humidity you either get a sprinkle or a monsoon downpour. An area of say 2 to 4 square foot should produce 6 gallons an hour at 60 to 80 percent humidity. Basically a unit the size of a water cooler should produce enough fresh water for a family of four. For tech specs you need around 6,000 to 10,000 volts, no more or it;'ll arc and less and the process stops. Spacing and a few other factors affect it so tweak the voltage until you see water droplets.

Now, run two mesh screens the length of a greenhouse and the width of your grow bed. My guess, a transformer should handle an area 10'X12' so 20 transformers can handle a 24'X100' greenhouse and remember they only need to run when you want to produce water so say every third day or three times a week? An hour or two of rain? I say a 1000 watts of solar panel and a decent back up solar battery. Just did the metal math, say $6,000 and it produces all the water to irrigate a 24'X100' greenhouse and I include sensors and say an arduino to control it but you can passively wire it and avoid the arduino. Just easier with it. Every 15 years replace the transformers.....

Try it. Run a charge through two pieces of mesh and you'll see water drops form on one of them. Why aren't they producing water this way? I can make a self filling canteen with little more than two pieces of stainless steel mesh and a bloody stungun. The joke is I'd have to probably step down the voltage but the cheapies are maybe weak enough. I have a couple. May have to try something. Wish I'm gotten my plasma lab built. Could have tested it in 15 minutes. What I planned was to set up an aquarium and put a mesh over half of it and show it rain by flipping a switch. I was just thinking it through, need to test again how long the stungun batteries last and scale up the batteries to say a portable drill battery? Got  curious how much water a say like a 40 watt hour battery would produce? Just picked up a battery that size and it;s maybe 3lbs, more than a kilo for sure. My point it that battery could fill a canteen maybe 5X or 10X and weigh a lot less than the water. River water unsafe? Sit by the river where it;s humid and fill your canteen. An African village could get everything from a 10' dome and if they have polluted water it would purify it as well as steam and only use one solar panel for the whole village. To whom it may concern, everything I do is under creative commons. Odds are there are patents in there already. Such a simple process a monkey could build one......

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
just a quick note on 3d printer accuracy...   in the Z (layers) any modern printer can easily do 0.08mm layer height.    
the default layer height is set to 0.2mm,  0.1 is often selected for 'fine', but it'll go lower if you ask it to.   
and then in the X/Y you generally (with a 0.4mm nozzle) have a (roughly)  0.46mm width of extrusion, so that'll limit details in the x/y plane. 
so.. you can get more details by printing your print sideways or diagonally sometimes. 
likewise, if it's easy to swap nozzles on your printer, swapping to a 0.2mm nozzle means your x/y will be around 0.25mm extrusions. 
and, of course, you can go to 0.6, 0.8 or larger nozzles, but there are other reasons for that and small details isn't one of them. :-) 
other quick note..  going from 0.2mm layer height to 0.1mm layer height.. while great for looks, doubles the print time. ;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the tips mate

replies.author: @CrudelyMade
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering it's the least I can do, for all the knowledge that you share with us. :-D

author: @psane
text: any idea how to do the same operations in Fusion 360?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: easiest way - use the spur gear script to create the gear then use the sweep at say 30 degrees along a defined line perpendicular to the spur gear

author: @AlanBell
text: Good to understand how to do it taking into consideration the layer heights. One alternative way is to use FreeCAD and the Gears workbench, you can create involute gears with many parameters, I like a beta angle of 10 degrees and a double helix, so you get herringbone gears. You can then export them from FreeCAD as STL files to import to Tinkercad if that is your more familiar design environment.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you can also do it in fusion 360 using the sweep function

author: @ZephPeterson
text:
You have sight I aspire to see throught. I believe in the inherent value of transient context entities. I believe in the inherent power derived from understanding the NeST of the NSeT in SeTN, and resulting conclusive awareness as a TCe that precedes 
Sent ence. Once 
Sent ence is achieved, further discourse can be built upon it. If this resonates at all with you...please just respond anything...i will document our combined tra sient nature in the Experiement (typos inte tion l)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am sorry mate but I don't really understand what you are saying - can you explain in more understandable way?

replies.author: @ZephPeterson
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  ah yes...my prime difficulty..direct concepts.. 
I am a weird retired cryptologist. One of the things you demonstrate in your videos is a remarkable ability to see nested patterns within the mechanical logic flow of your excellent creations. Its not really an easy concept, nor really fun unless you have a dented brain, but the essence of what i am getting as is: nesting as a universal pattern explains all iterations in the set that defines existing things. Your application of this principle is particularly clear, based on the thoroughness of detail in your content. I draw inspiration from the pain i know you must be aware of, and the way you channel that into productive energy for the unified human collective. I know time and mental effort is valuable...but i am hoping someone finds this tedious read worth it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @ZephPeterson ah yes - now I understand what you mean - thank you for taking the time to expand on your initial comment mate. Yes - the same patterns repeat themselves almost in a fractal way - once you start to see those patterns they occur across all fields - i have found striking parallels in languages - I speak, german, spanish, italian, turkish, russian and a little japanese and what you are talking about occurs across these languages for certain and in mechanics, maths and chemistry so I suspect them of being universals

replies.author: @ZephPeterson
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  exactly! Privyet! Ahalin wasahalin, caio, ne how..ad naseum! The fractal NeST! I propose a collection of interacting energy is similar accross all scales, with inverting properties( energetic repulsion vs  gravitational attraction) occuring in alternating iterations, defining the basic wave function/point mechanics interplay. It goes even further if you consider any motion within a space likely influences the time within that space. 
The linguistical puzzle is to determine your level of pattern awareness. What is the word..SeNT eNTS? Each are nested components rearranged to form a new thing by creatively using a blank space.
